target.title: fix: WebChat duplicates external reply events
target.body: Related: #85771 ## What Problem This Solves Fixes a WebChat-only duplicate reply bug where an externally triggered turn, such as a WeChat/AKK event, could render two identical assistant bubbles even though the durable transcript already had only one assistant message for that turn. The duplication was visible in WebChat after live streaming completed. The affected session could receive the same assistant answer through two UI data paths: - `chat.history` refresh already returned the persisted assistant message for the current turn. - The still-active live stream later delivered the terminal `final` event for the same assistant answer. Before this fix, WebChat appended the terminal final message even when the same visible assistant reply had already arrived through the refreshed history snapshot. ## Why This Change Was Made The final fix keeps the reconciliation in Control UI, where the duplicate is created: - `appendTerminalAssistantMessage` now treats terminal assistant finals as replacements for matching assistant messages already present after the latest user turn. - The matching is based on assistant-visible content. Hidden reasoning/thinking blocks are ignored, while visible non-text blocks, such as canvas-style blocks, keep the full content signature to avoid over-deduping rich replies. - If the active text stream has already been persisted into history, the terminal final replaces that history/stream placeholder instead of appending a second bubble. - `loadChatHistory` now drops a late optimistic assistant tail only when the refreshed current history turn already replaces that assistant text. It preserves legitimate repeated assistant replies from later user turns. This avoids changing transcript persistence, plugin behavior, WeChat/AKK delivery, or gateway event semantics. ## User Impact WebChat no longer displays duplicate assistant bubbles when a refreshed history snapshot and the live terminal event both contain the same current-turn assistant reply. Legitimate repeated assistant text is still preserved when it belongs to a distinct later user turn or includes visible non-text content that should render as a separate reply. ## Evidence ### Automated validation ```text pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 140 passed (140) ``` ```text pnpm build ``` ```text pnpm format:check ui/src/ui/chat/stream-reconciliation.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ``` ```text node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/stream-reconciliation.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ``` ```text git diff --check ``` ```text .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported overall: patch is correct (0.8) ``` `pnpm check:changed` was attempted, but the local Crabbox/Testbox wrapper failed before running project checks: ```text [crabbox] selected binary failed basic --version/--help sanity checks ``` ### Manual WebChat repro proof The following logs are redacted console proof from the affected WebChat session. They use counts, run ids, sequence numbers, and content hashes only; they do not include message content. #### Before fix WebChat history had already applied the persisted assistant answer for the current turn, then the terminal final event appended another visible bubble: ```text history before apply: status=before-history-apply sessionKey=agent:main:openclaw-weixin:direct:[wechat-contact]@im.wechat responseTail included assistant:persisted:len=474:hash=3d69233e live terminal final: runId=899794e0-5809-40c1-b882-3054c7fdc28f seq=275 state=final before final: chatMessages=100, chatStreamChars=474 after final: chatMessages=101, chatStreamChars=0 ``` The `chatMessages` count increasing from `100` to `101` after the final event is the duplicate WebChat append. #### After fix With this patch, the same current-turn shape reconciles the persisted history assistant and the terminal final instead of appending a second bubble: ```text history before apply: status=before-history-apply sessionKey=agent:main:openclaw-weixin:direct:[wechat-contact]@im.wechat responseTail included assistant:persisted:len=455:hash=230f659f live terminal final: runId=1c3143ee-c6ec-42fd-8bab-becf615a84bc seq=254 state=final before final: chatMessages=100, chatStreamChars=455 after final: chatMessages=100, chatStreamChars=0 ``` The `chatMessages` count staying at `100` after the final event proves WebChat did not append the duplicate terminal bubble. ### Screenshot slots - Before fix duplicate WebChat reply: - After fix single WebChat reply: ### Regression coverage added The focused tests cover: - terminal final already present in the current turn - visible text already present from refreshed history - hidden reasoning/thinking blocks ignored for duplicate matching - active stream text already persisted by history - partial live assistant tail replaced by current history - repeated assistant text after a new user turn preserved - repeated assistant text with visible non-text content preserved

commands.author: idootop

target.title: feat(xiaomi): expose MiMo V2.5 on pay-as-you-go and default to mimo-v2.5
target.body:
Refs #87277

## What Problem This Solves

Pay-as-you-go Xiaomi users could not select Xiaomi's current model generation.
The `xiaomi` provider (pay-as-you-go, `https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1`) only
listed the legacy MiMo V2 catalog (`mimo-v2-flash`, `mimo-v2-pro`,
`mimo-v2-omni`) and defaulted to `mimo-v2-flash`, even though the same endpoint
already serves the MiMo V2.5 generation. Only `xiaomi-token-plan` listed V2.5.
During onboarding and model selection, pay-as-you-go users were steered to a
retired default and could not pick `mimo-v2.5` / `mimo-v2.5-pro` at all.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `mimo-v2.5` to the `xiaomi` pay-as-you-go catalog
(ahead of the legacy V2 rows) and changes **both** the pay-as-you-go and Token
Plan defaults to `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5`. Xiaomi's deprecation docs map the retired
`mimo-v2-flash` to `mimo-v2.5` (not the higher-priced `-pro` tier), so the
default follows Xiaomi's documented replacement. The alias derivation in both
onboarding presets is aligned to use the model display name. Legacy `mimo-v2-*`
rows stay selectable for continuity and are marked superseded in docs.

**MiMo V2.5 omni note (scoping):** `mimo-v2.5` is natively an omni model
(text, image, audio, video). The bundled plugin manifest `modelCatalog` input
set is limited to `text` / `image` / `document` by `MODEL_CATALOG_INPUTS` in
`@openclaw/model-catalog-core`
(`packages/model-catalog-core/src/model-catalog-normalize.ts:32`) — `audio` /
`video` declared in a manifest row are dropped during normalization. So this PR
advertises `mimo-v2.5` as `text, image` to match runtime reality and documents
the `openclaw.json` `models` path for audio/video (the user-config path does
accept all four via `zod-schema.core.ts`). Advertising native omni from the
bundled catalog needs a separate core change.

### Issue #87277 checklist status

| Item | Status |
|---|---|
| Add `mimo-v2.5` to built-in Xiaomi catalog | Done (both `xiaomi` and `xiaomi-token-plan`) |
| Add `mimo-v2.5` to onboarding model selection | Done (catalog drives the picker; now the default) |
| Update docs with V2.5 catalog row | Done (`docs/providers/xiaomi.md`) |
| Migration note: V2.5 supersedes V2-Omni | Done (docs note + default → `mimo-v2.5`) |
| `input: ["text","image","audio","video"]` in catalog | Partial — blocked on `MODEL_CATALOG_INPUTS` (see note); advertised as `text, image` |
| `multimodalFallback` config key | Out of scope (core agent-routing feature, not provider metadata) |
| Pre-dispatch input-type inspection + history injection | Out of scope (same core routing feature) |

## User Impact

- Pay-as-you-go onboarding now defaults to `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` (Xiaomi's
  documented `mimo-v2-flash` replacement) and offers `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro`.
- `openclaw models list --provider xiaomi` lists the V2.5 rows.
- Existing configs pinning a `mimo-v2-*` model keep working; those rows remain
  in the catalog.

## Model parameters

Pricing = Xiaomi's published overseas pay-as-you-go USD/1M tokens
(https://mimo.mi.com/docs/zh-CN/price/pay-as-you-go), cross-checked against
https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5 , https://mimo.mi.com/models/mimo-v2.5-pro
and the live OpenRouter models API (https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models).

| Model | Input | Context | Max output | Reasoning | input $ | output $ | cacheRead $ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `mimo-v2.5` | text, image | 1,048,576 | 131,072 | Yes | 0.14 | 0.28 | 0.0028 |
| `mimo-v2.5-pro` | text | 1,048,576 | 131,072 | Yes | 0.435 | 0.87 | 0.0036 |

Pay-as-you-go has no context-length tiered pricing (unlike Token Plan), so no
`tieredPricing` is set. `mimo-v2.5-pro`'s `0.435/0.87/0.0036` match OpenRouter
exactly.

## Evidence

**After-fix real CLI — `openclaw models list --provider xiaomi`** (static catalog, no key needed):

```
Model                                      Input      Ctx         Local Auth  Tags
xiaomi/mimo-v2-flash                       text       256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-omni                        text+image 256k        no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2-pro                         text       1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5                           text+image 1024k       no    no
xiaomi/mimo-v2.5-pro                       text       1024k       no    no
```

**After-fix real onboarding/default** — `openclaw onboard --auth-choice xiaomi-api-key --xiaomi-api-key sk-REDACTED --non-interactive --accept-risk` in an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, generated `openclaw.json`:

```
primary: {"primary": "xiaomi/mimo-v2.5"}
aliases: {"xiaomi/mimo-v2.5": {"alias": "Xiaomi MiMo V2.5"}}
```

Token Plan onboarding (`--auth-choice xiaomi-token-plan-sgp`) likewise resolves
the default to `xiaomi-token-plan/mimo-v2.5`.

**Focused tests** — `pnpm test extensions/xiaomi/onboard.test.ts extensions/xiaomi/index.test.ts` → 23 passed. They assert the exact pay-as-you-go catalog order, reasoning flags, `mimo-v2.5` resolving to `["text","image"]` from the manifest, and the new `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` default + `Xiaomi MiMo V2.5` alias for both providers.

target.title: [codex] Bound memory flush append payloads
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90354.

This PR adds a conservative runtime guard to memory-triggered append-only writes when the target is the canonical daily memory file (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`):

- rejects oversized daily-memory flush append payloads before mutation
- rejects markdown heading / daily-memory scaffold lines before mutation
- skips exact duplicate lines that are already present
- appends only the remaining compact new lines
- keeps both pre-append duplicate reads and final appends on the same fs-safe root boundary
- returns structured `memoryFlushAppend` details for accepted and skipped writes
- documents the bounded daily-memory append behavior

## What Problem This Solves

The existing memory-flush write wrapper correctly restricts writes to the planned append-only target path, but it still accepted arbitrary string content. That left the runtime able to append long generated narrative blocks, duplicated daily-memory scaffolds, or repeated read-back lines into `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`, with downstream guards only catching the pollution after mutation.

This PR moves the first line of defense into the existing runtime append wrapper. It prevents the common bad payload shapes before the file is mutated, while keeping the memory-flush surface narrow and avoiding a new command-execution or validator-hook boundary.

## Behavior

For memory-flush writes to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`, the wrapper now:

- allows at most 3 new non-empty lines
- allows at most 800 chars total for the new append payload
- allows at most 500 chars per line
- rejects markdown headings such as daily-memory scaffolds
- de-dupes normalized exact lines against existing file content
- reads existing daily-memory content through `fsRoot(root).read(...)` so symlink and hardlink aliases are rejected before duplicate detection
- leaves the file untouched on rejection or duplicate-only skips

For non-daily memory-flush targets, the existing append-only behavior is preserved.

## Security / Product Boundary

The issue-side ClawSweeper review called out the security risk of a broad post-write command hook. This PR deliberately avoids adding a shell-style validator hook, post-write command, rollback command, plugin API, or new config surface. It implements built-in conservative daily-memory bounds at the already-existing append wrapper boundary.

## Evidence

Local validation on the PR branch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts` -> 2 files passed, 22 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts` -> pass
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/gateway/config-agents.md` -> pass, docs formatting clean
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` -> pass
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts` -> pass
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> pass
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` -> pass

Behavior covered by regression tests:

- accepted memory-flush appends now return structured `memoryFlushAppend` telemetry
- oversized daily-memory flush lines are rejected before mutation
- daily-memory scaffold / markdown heading payloads are rejected before mutation
- duplicate-only daily-memory appends skip without mutation
- mixed duplicate + new-line payloads append only the compact new line
- symlink and hardlink aliases at the daily-memory path are rejected before mutation
- embedded memory-flush forwarding still activates the append-only wrapper and reports the new telemetry

## Real Behavior Proof

Redacted local runtime proof on the PR branch at `5871ae86c58270cbd871b4191b1977893ba578e9`. The harness imports the exported runtime pieces directly: `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` / `wrapToolMemoryFlushAppendOnlyWrite` from `src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts`, and `buildEmbeddedAttemptToolRunContext` from `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.tool-run-context.ts`.

```text
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module --eval [redacted live memory-flush append harness]
head: {"commit":"5871ae86c58270cbd871b4191b1977893ba578e9","branch":"codex/fix-90354-memory-flush-bounds"}
embedded_context: {"trigger":"memory","memoryFlushWritePath":"memory/2026-06-24.md"}
accepted: {"details":{"path":"memory/2026-06-24.md","appendOnly":true,"memoryFlushAppend":{"status":"accepted","appendedLines":1,"appendChars":29,"skippedDuplicateLines":0}},"fileHash":"1c3d660ebe6c"}
duplicate_skip: {"details":{"path":"memory/2026-06-24.md","appendOnly":true,"memoryFlushAppend":{"status":"skipped_duplicate","skippedDuplicateLines":1}},"unchanged":true,"fileHash":"1c3d660ebe6c"}
scaffold_rejected: {"ok":true,"message":"Memory flush append rejected: markdown headings or daily-memory scaffolds are not allowed; append only new note lines.","unchanged":true,"fileHash":"1c3d660ebe6c"}
oversized_rejected: {"ok":true,"message":"Memory flush append rejected: line too long (522 chars; max 500). Write a short pointer instead of a transcript-style narrative.","unchanged":true,"fileHash":"1c3d660ebe6c"}
final_file: {"redactedPath":"$TMP/openclaw-memory-flush-proof-*/workspace/memory/2026-06-24.md","lines":2,"content":"- existing durable note\n- accepted compact proof note"}
```

This covers the ClawSweeper proof ask: accepted compact append, duplicate-only skip without mutation, and rejected scaffold/oversized payloads before mutation. The proof also shows the memory-triggered embedded-attempt context carrying the daily-memory write target into tool construction.

## Compatibility / Upgrade Behavior

The stricter default is intentional for the canonical daily-memory memory-flush target only: `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This is not a serialized state migration and it does not rewrite existing memory files. Non-daily memory-flush append targets keep the existing append-only behavior. For daily-memory flushes, noisy generated payloads now fail closed or skip duplicates before mutation so durable memory is not polluted.

Maintainers should explicitly confirm this strict default is acceptable before merge. If they prefer a default-compatible rollout, the policy can be moved behind an opt-in later, but this PR deliberately implements the fail-closed behavior requested by #90354.

target.title: [codex] active-memory default to configured agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Default Active Memory to configured OpenClaw agents when `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` is omitted.
- Fall back to the implicit `main` agent for minimal configs that do not define `agents.list`.
- Preserve explicit empty `config.agents: []` as no targeted agents.
- Preserve explicit non-empty `config.agents` allowlist behavior.
- Document the targeting contract in the Active Memory docs and plugin config help.

This matters because omitting `config.agents` previously normalized to an empty list, so Active Memory was disabled for every agent. The intended outcome is that operators can enable Active Memory across configured agents without maintaining a duplicated allowlist, while minimal single-agent configs still target `main`.

Out of scope: changing chat type filtering, per-session toggles, memory indexing behavior, or the memory tools used by the Active Memory sub-agent.

Review focus: whether the fallback to `agents.list[].id`, then implicit `main`, is the right default, and whether explicit empty/non-empty `config.agents` remains unchanged.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #82528

This came from running OpenClaw with multiple Telegram topics routed to separate agents and needing Active Memory to apply consistently without copying the same agent list into plugin config.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed:
  Omitting `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` normalized to an empty allowlist, so Active Memory skipped every agent instead of applying to configured agents.
- Real environment tested:
  OpenClaw 2026.6.5 on a Linux host with a systemd user gateway, Telegram group topics routed to `main`, `sementes`, `cloudfarm`, and `pedro-agenda`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  Applied the equivalent runtime change locally, removed `active-memory.config.agents`, restarted `openclaw-gateway`, then checked gateway status and memory search.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw gateway status --deep
Gateway version: 2026.6.5
Runtime: running
Capability: admin-capable
```

```text
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "3 minutes ago"
[gateway] http server listening (12 plugins: active-memory, brave, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, openai, phone-control, talk-voice, telegram; 12.1s)
```

```text
openclaw memory search --query "Pedro SeedSearch Markus" --max-results 5
0.545 memory/pendencias-grupo.md:1-21
0.516 memory/2026-03-31.md:1-37
0.453 memory/dreaming/light/2026-06-05.md:43-72
```

- Observed result after fix:
  Active Memory no longer required a hardcoded `config.agents` list to apply across configured agents.
- What was not tested:
  A fresh live Telegram command round-trip was not captured on the final PR branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The production server was restored to upstream behavior after proof collection, using an explicit `config.agents` list until this PR is merged upstream.
- Before evidence:
  Reading the prior plugin implementation showed `normalizePluginConfig({}).agents` became `[]`, while `isEnabledForAgent` requires `config.agents.includes(agentId)`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
pnpm docs:list
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/active-memory.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check
node --import tsx -e "import { testing } from './extensions/active-memory/index.ts'; const cfg={agents:{list:[{id:'main'},{id:'support'}]}}; console.log(JSON.stringify({ omitted: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}, cfg).agents, empty: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents: []}, cfg).agents, explicit: testing.normalizePluginConfig({agents:['support']}, cfg).agents, implicitMain: testing.normalizePluginConfig({}).agents }, null, 2));"
git diff --check upstream/main -- docs/concepts/active-memory.md extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts extensions/active-memory/openclaw.plugin.json
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode commit --commit HEAD
```

Regression coverage added:

- `defaults to all configured agents when no active-memory agent allowlist is set`
- `preserves an empty active-memory agent allowlist`
- `defaults to the implicit main agent when no agent list is configured`
- `keeps an explicit active-memory agent allowlist`

CI after the latest push:

- Pending for commit `b7e75ed8eaa7`. Prior CI was green on `fdf408d1e55464aced878f0fee85201324590de3`.

Final-head normalization proof on `b7e75ed8eaa7`:

```json
{
  "omitted": [
    "main",
    "support"
  ],
  "empty": [],
  "explicit": [
    "support"
  ],
  "implicitMain": [
    "main"
  ]
}
```

Autoreview note:

- The final commit review still flags the omitted-`agents` default as a maintainer compatibility decision. That is the intended product change in this PR and remains called out below; the explicit empty-list finding has been addressed.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

Highest-risk area:
Operators who intentionally omitted `config.agents` expecting Active Memory to run nowhere would now get Active Memory for configured agents, or `main` in minimal configs. Explicit `config.agents: []` continues to target no agents.

Risk mitigation:
Explicit `config.agents: []` remains a no-target allowlist, and explicit non-empty `config.agents` still behaves as an allowlist. Operators can set `enabled: false`, disable the plugin entry, set `agents: []`, or provide a narrow `agents` list when they want less coverage. The docs and manifest help now state the omitted default and the explicit-empty behavior.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await maintainer compatibility decision for omitted `config.agents` and CI completion on the latest commit.

Still waiting on:
GitHub PR checks for the latest push and maintainer decision on the omitted-agent default policy.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed:
Filled in real behavior proof after the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` label was applied, then added docs/manifest alignment and focused regression coverage for omitted, explicit-empty, implicit-main, and explicit allowlist targeting. The latest update preserves explicit `agents: []` semantics per ClawSweeper guidance.

target.title: fix(cli): accept parent options placed after lazy subcommands (#55563 regression 1) [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> [AI-assisted] This PR was generated using Claude Code.

## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw browser tabs --browser-profile remote` (parent option placed *after* the subcommand) fails with `error: unknown option '--browser-profile'`. The pre-v2026.3.24 syntax `openclaw browser --browser-profile remote tabs` still works, so this is a silent regression of the option-ordering flexibility that existing WSL2/CDP documentation relies on. Regression 1 of #55563.
- **Why it matters now:** Every user following the existing browser/CDP troubleshooting docs gets an error that looks like a config problem, not a CLI syntax change. Reported by a contributor whose merged PR #39407 still documents the old form.
- **Intended outcome:** A parent option placed after a lazy subcommand is accepted again, matching the pre-lazy behavior and the existing root-option normalization (`--no-color`, `--log-level` after subcommands).
- **Out of scope:** Regression 2 of #55563 (gateway cycling after `openclaw doctor --fix`) is a separate code path already tracked by open PR #94148; this PR does not touch `doctor`.
- **Success look:** `openclaw browser tabs --browser-profile remote` parses and reaches the `tabs` action with `browserProfile === "remote"`, identical to `openclaw browser --browser-profile remote tabs`.
- **Reviewer focus:** `hoistParentOptionsBeforeSubcommand` in `src/cli/program/helpers.ts` and its single call site in `src/cli/program/action-reparse.ts`. The hoist only moves tokens that match a parent/ancestor command's own option flags from after the subcommand to before it; it is a no-op when parent options already precede the subcommand, so working invocations are unchanged.

## Linked context

Addresses Regression 1 of #55563 (the issue bundles two regressions; only the browser CLI syntax break is fixed here).

Related #55563 (Regression 2 — `doctor --fix` gateway cycling — tracked separately by #94148). Also related to the prior root-option-after-subcommand fixes #93455 (`--log-level`) and the `--no-color` normalization, which established the argv-normalization pattern this extends to parent-command options.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw browser tabs --browser-profile remote` rejected `--browser-profile` as an unknown option because the lazy reparse replays the original argv verbatim and the real `tabs` subcommand does not define (or allow) the parent's `--browser-profile`.
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node v22.22.3, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw built from source on branch `fix/vuln-55563`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the real CLI `node ./openclaw.mjs browser tabs --browser-profile remote` and the lazy-reparse integration test in `extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts` (real Commander parse + real lazy reparse; only the browser network modules are stubbed, the option-parsing path under fix is real).
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```
$ node ./openclaw.mjs browser tabs --browser-profile remote   (real CLI, fix applied)
(no "unknown option" error — parse accepts --browser-profile after the subcommand;
 command proceeds into the tabs action. No browser gateway is running in this env,
 so it does not list tabs, but the option-ordering regression is resolved.)

$ pnpm test -- extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts   (fix applied)
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
  accepts --browser-profile placed after the lazy subcommand (#55563 regression 1)
    -> tabsAction called once; browser parent opts browserProfile === "remote"
```
- Observed result after fix: The parent option `--browser-profile remote` placed after the `tabs` subcommand is accepted; the `tabs` action runs with `browserProfile === "remote"`, identical to placing the option before the subcommand. A bare `--browser-profile` with no following value (or followed by another flag like `--json`) is left in place, so Commander still reports the missing-value/unknown-option error and the `tabs` action does not run — the bare flag is never hoisted alone (which would let Commander consume the next token as the value).
```
$ node ./openclaw.mjs browser tabs --browser-profile   (real CLI, fix applied)
error: unknown option '--browser-profile'
(missing-value path preserved: bare parent value option is NOT hoisted alone,
 so the subcommand name is never swallowed as the option value)

$ pnpm test -- src/cli/program/helpers.test.ts extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts   (fix applied)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  57 passed (57)
  ✓ does not hoist a bare value option with no following value
  ✓ does not hoist a value option followed by another flag
  ✓ does not hoist an empty --flag= form
  ✓ preserves the missing-value error for a bare --browser-profile after the subcommand (#55563 regression 1)
  ✓ preserves the missing-value error when --browser-profile is followed by another flag (#55563 regression 1)
```
- What was not tested: macOS/Linux, non-browser lazy command trees with deeply nested subcommands beyond the two-level `browser tab new` shape, and the actual tab listing against a live browser gateway (no browser running in this environment).
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The before/after contrast is captured through the real lazy-reparse path exercised by the integration test (real Commander + real reparse + real hoist; browser network modules stubbed) and corroborated by the real `openclaw` CLI accepting the option. A live browser gateway was not available locally.
- Before evidence:
```
$ pnpm test -- extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts   (fix reverted, HEAD~1)
error: unknown option '--browser-profile'
 |extension-browser| extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts (8 tests | 1 failed)
  x accepts --browser-profile placed after the lazy subcommand (#55563 regression 1)
Error: process.exit unexpectedly called with "1"
  Command.error node_modules/commander/lib/command.js:1957:10
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  1 failed | 7 passed (8)
```

## Tests and validation

- Commands run: `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` (changed files clean), `pnpm tsgo` (exit 0), `pnpm exec oxlint --type-aware` on changed files (exit 0), `pnpm build` (exit 0, including `write-cli-startup-metadata` postbuild), `pnpm test -- src/cli/program/helpers.test.ts src/cli/program/action-reparse.test.ts` (47 + 6 pass), `pnpm test -- extensions/browser/src/cli/browser-cli.lazy.test.ts` (10 pass).
- Regression coverage added: `hoistParentOptionsBeforeSubcommand` unit tests (`helpers.test.ts`, 11 cases: value/boolean hoist, `--flag=value`, no-op when already before, subcommand-own-option preserved, root-option skip, root-parent no-op, subcommand-not-found no-op, plus 3 missing-value preservation cases — bare value option, value option followed by another flag, empty `--flag=` form); an `action-reparse.test.ts` case asserting the hoisted argv is re-parsed; and `browser-cli.lazy.test.ts` cases asserting `browser tabs --browser-profile remote` reaches the `tabs` action with `browserProfile === "remote"` (fails on `HEAD~1` with `unknown option '--browser-profile'`) and that bare `browser tabs --browser-profile` / `browser tabs --browser-profile --json` preserve the missing-value error (the `tabs` action does not run).
- What failed before this fix: `openclaw browser tabs --browser-profile remote` exited with `error: unknown option '--browser-profile'`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes — a previously-rejected option placement (`browser tabs --browser-profile remote`) is now accepted, restoring pre-v2026.3.24 behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. No CODEOWNERS secops paths are touched.

What is the highest-risk area? The shared lazy-reparse path (`reparseProgramFromActionArgs`) is used by every lazy CLI command, so a hoist bug could affect other commands.

How is that risk mitigated? The hoist is strictly conservative: it only relocates tokens that match a parent/ancestor command's own option flags (value vs boolean resolved from Commander `Option.required`/`optional`), excludes options the subcommand itself defines, stops at `--`, and returns argv unchanged when no parent option appears after the subcommand. Working invocations (parent options before the subcommand) are untouched (hoisted array is empty, early return). New unit + integration tests cover the no-op and hoist paths; existing lazy-command tests are unchanged by the fix (pre-existing Windows-only failures in `run-main.exit`/`update-cli` fail identically on clean `main`).

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review and CI run.

Addressed in the follow-up push (commit `83bf4222dc`): the ClawSweeper/Codex P2 finding — `consumeHoistableOption` no longer hoists a parent value option that has no following value. A bare `--browser-profile` (or `--browser-profile --json`, or `--browser-profile=`) is left in place, preserving Commander's missing-value error instead of swallowing the next token as the option value. Added 3 `helpers.test.ts` and 2 `browser-cli.lazy.test.ts` regression cases for the missing-value paths; reran the focused lazy CLI tests, oxlint on touched files, and `git diff --check` (all clean).

Still waiting on: maintainer review; the heavy CI shards (which require maintainer `Authorize workflow actor` approval for first-time contributor workflow runs) and the automated Real behavior proof / ClawSweeper re-review.

Bot or reviewer comments addressed: ClawSweeper/Codex P2 finding "Preserve missing parent option errors" (`src/cli/program/helpers.ts:172`) — fixed and covered by regression tests as described above.

target.title: fix(telegram): explain disabled plugin approval failures
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes #95800.

Telegram-originated plugin approvals, such as `skill_workshop apply`, could fail with a generic no-route or timeout message when Telegram native approvals were unavailable because `channels.telegram.execApprovals.approvers` and `commands.ownerAllowFrom` were not configured. The Telegram setup guidance already existed, but the agent-facing plugin approval failure did not surface it.

## Why This Change Was Made
The approval hook already receives the turn source. This PR updates the agent-side plugin approval no-route and timeout failure paths so they can append channel setup guidance when the initiating channel's plugin approval route is disabled and that channel exposes plugin-specific setup text.

The setup text is now plugin-surface aware. Split-route channels with exec setup text but no plugin setup capability keep the generic plugin approval failure instead of receiving misleading exec setup instructions.

This keeps the current approver-gating model intact and does not auto-route, auto-approve, or enable Telegram approvals.

## User Impact
Telegram-only operators get an actionable explanation telling them to use Web UI or terminal UI for now, or configure `channels.telegram.execApprovals.approvers` / `commands.ownerAllowFrom`, instead of a silent timeout-shaped denial.

Operators on split-route channels are not told to enable exec approvals for plugin approval failures unless the channel explicitly exposes plugin approval setup guidance.

## Evidence
- Current head: `858a7b2dda28a07982af45e01caad1b50644765b`.
- Out-of-scope follow-up code commit `34e3c10e23ec8d3cf00ff803188df4226cd60f8d` was reverted by `e924d2813c19d808fa1a583da03b10b642d162f2`; this body describes only the current PR diff.

- Real behavior proof on the current head:

```text
node --import tsx --input-type=module <stdin>
```

Run setup:

```text
spawned a real isolated OpenClaw Gateway child from dist/index.js
staged the Telegram plugin
set skills.workshop.approvalPolicy=pending
set channels.telegram.enabled=true
left channels.telegram.execApprovals.approvers unset
set commands.ownerAllowFrom=[]
called runBeforeToolCallHook for skill_workshop apply
set turnSourceChannel=telegram and turnSourceAccountId=default
actual path: runBeforeToolCallHook -> requestPluginToolApproval -> gateway plugin.approval.request
```

Observed output:

```text
OpenClaw PR #95973 real behavior proof
head=858a7b2dda28a07982af45e01caad1b50644765b
gateway=real child pid=12130 baseUrl=http://127.0.0.1:60953
path=runBeforeToolCallHook -> requestPluginToolApproval -> gateway plugin.approval.request
turnSourceChannel=telegram turnSourceAccountId=default
approvalSurface=telegram enabled with no approvers and empty commands.ownerAllowFrom
result.blocked=true
result.reason:
Plugin approval unavailable (no approval route)

Approve it from the Web UI or terminal UI for now. Telegram supports native exec approvals for this account. Configure `channels.telegram.execApprovals.approvers` or `commands.ownerAllowFrom`; leave `channels.telegram.execApprovals.enabled` unset/`auto` or set it to `true`.
```

This is not a unit-test-only proof: the run used the real agent approval hook and real Gateway plugin approval request path. The provider was `mock-openai`, which is not involved in plugin approval routing.

- Focused regression proof on the current head:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-surface.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.test.ts src/channels/plugins/approvals.test.ts
```

Result: 4 Vitest shards passed, 83 tests passed.

- The focused coverage asserts:
  - Telegram-originated plugin no-route and timeout failures include the setup guidance above.
  - A split-route channel with exec setup text but no plugin setup capability does not reuse exec setup guidance for plugin approval failures.
  - The plugin SDK helper carries the optional plugin setup capability through the approval capability shape.
  - The channel approval adapter and approval surface helper still resolve the expected capability surfaces.

- Formatting, types, build, and diff checks on the current head:

```text
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-surface.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-delivery-helpers.ts src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.packages.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-packages.tsbuildinfo
pnpm build
git diff --check
```

Result: all passed.

- Autoreview note: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local ...` could not start because the local Codex CLI config has `service_tier=default`, which this helper rejects as an unknown variant. It failed before reviewing code; no autoreview findings were produced.

## Notes

- Issue assignment to `@steipete` also failed from this token with GitHub 403.

target.title: fix(video-generation): bound dashscope task response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` reads two provider-controlled
response bodies (the submit-task response and the poll-status response) with
raw `await response.json()` calls. That call has no byte cap, so a faulty or
hostile DashScope endpoint streaming an unbounded body can force the runtime
to buffer the whole payload before parsing — an OOM / DoS surface in the
production path. The download path on line 338 already uses
`readResponseWithLimit`; this brings the submit and poll paths into the
same bound-read family.

The shared `readProviderJsonResponse` helper in
`openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` (used by the bound-stream family merged
in #95218, #95417, #95418, #95420, #95246, #96136) caps the read at 16 MiB
and surfaces an actionable error before OOM. This PR routes both the
submit and poll DashScope paths through that helper.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Cap is symmetric with the download path already in the same file.
- Bounded-read helper is generic, owned by `provider-http`, and already
  proven on `provider-http-errors.ts` and the image-generation/video-
  generation sibling surfaces.
- Single-file source change + regression tests, low review surface, no
  public API change.

## Changes

- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` — `+11/-2` — import
  `readProviderJsonResponse` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`;
  replace two raw `await response.json()` calls (line 203, the poll-status
  path, and line 273, the submit-task path) with
  `readProviderJsonResponse<DashscopeVideoGenerationResponse>(response,
  label)`.
- `src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts` —
  `+181/-0` — new test file. 4 unit tests covering: well-formed poll
  parse, overflow on the poll path (stops at 17/32 chunks for a 32 MiB
  body), well-formed submit path parse, **and** overflow on the submit
  path via `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` (directly exercises the
  submit function ClawSweeper's previous review flagged as "not
  directly proven").
- `scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — `+114/-0` — new
  real-environment repro driving the production
  `pollDashscopeVideoTaskUntilComplete` with a 32 MiB streaming body
  and proving the bounded reader stops at 17/32 chunks. Includes a
  **negative control** showing that raw `response.json()` on the same
  body buffers the full 32 MiB before failing on parse.

## Evidence

### Real-environment repro output

```
$ pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs
=== Reproduction for dashscope-compatible bounded JSON read ===
PROVIDER_JSON_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES = 16777216 bytes

PASS  bounded read: threw "dashscope video-generation task poll: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes" after 17 / 32 chunks (stopped ~17 MiB into a 32 MiB body)
PASS  negative control: raw response.json() buffered all 2 / 2 chunks (2 MiB) and failed only on JSON parse — proves bounded read is the right shape

=== All repro assertions passed ===
```

### Unit tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

The 4th test ("surfaces a verbatim bounded-reader error from the submit
path") calls `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` end-to-end with a 100
MiB streaming body on the submit response and asserts the bounded
reader throws with the canonical overflow error and stops pulling
chunks well before the body completes.

## ClawSweeper finding addressed in this revision

The previous review's blocker said "the branch is not merge-ready
because it misses the current provider-http test mock contract and
does not actually exercise the submit helper it changes."

- Submit helper is now directly exercised by the new submit-path test
  that calls `runDashscopeVideoGenerationTask` and asserts the
  verbatim overflow error from the bounded reader.
- The bounded reader is the same one exposed via
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` (`readProviderJsonResponse`),
  so the test exercises the canonical helper, not a local copy.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.bounded-json.test.ts`
  — 4/4 passed
- `pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/issue-dashscope-response-cap.mjs` — 2/2 PASS
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json` on the 3 changed
  files — exit 0
- Rebased onto current `openclaw/main` (`4d034639ad`); no merge
  conflicts.


## CI status note

The most recent run reported one failing check
(`checks-node-compact-large-1` /
`src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts` /
`acquireSessionWriteLock > retries when a stale lock report is
replaced by a fresh payload-less lock`). This failure is in the
session write-lock retry path, which shares no surface with the
video-generation change in this PR. The PR only touches
`src/video-generation/dashscope-compatible.ts` and its test/repro
files; the failing test exercises `acquireSessionWriteLock` in
`src/agents/session-write-lock.ts`, an unrelated module. A re-run
should clear it.

## Out of scope

- Sibling surfaces in `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (4 sites),
  `src/infra/clawhub.ts` (3 sites), and others are intentionally
  left for follow-up PRs.
- The `readResponseWithLimit` download path is already bounded in this
  file and does not need changes.

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: feat: add interactive launcher menu as thin wrapper over existing CLI
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Users need a simple interactive menu to launch OpenClaw without remembering CLI commands. This PR adds a thin wrapper launcher that provides a friendly menu UX while delegating all operations to existing audited CLI commands.

## Why This Change Was Made

The launcher provides a single double-click entry point for common operations (TUI, WebUI, update, gateway management) without duplicating any existing OpenClaw surfaces. All sensitive operations remain in the existing audited code paths.

## User Impact

- **Interactive menu**: Run launcher to see TUI/WebUI/Update/Gateway options
- **No security risks**: Token handling remains in `openclaw dashboard`, no plaintext storage
- **No destructive operations**: Update delegates to `openclaw update` safe path
- **Proper service management**: Gateway uses `openclaw gateway install` (launchd/systemd/schtasks)

## Evidence

### Delegation Map
| Launcher Menu | Delegated To |
|--------------|--------------|
| [1] TUI | `openclaw tui` |
| [2] WebUI | `openclaw dashboard` |
| [3] Update | `openclaw update` |
| [4] Gateway | `openclaw gateway start/stop/restart/install` |

### What This PR Adds
- `launcher/src/main.rs` (347 lines) - Thin wrapper with interactive menu
- `launcher/README.md` - Documentation
- `launcher/Cargo.toml` - Rust project config
- `package.json` - Added `launcher:build` script

### What This PR Does NOT Do
- No plaintext token storage
- No custom git/pnpm operations
- No child-process gateway spawning
- No duplicate macOS packaging
- No custom updater logic

All operations delegate to existing canonical `openclaw` CLI commands.

commands.author: BSG2000

target.title: feat(ui): inline rename in the in-chat session picker
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The in-chat session picker only lets users *switch* sessions. Renaming still requires the admin "Sessions" tab, which is cumbersome when juggling several parallel, topic-specific chats — exactly the friction reported in #54397.
- **Change:** Add a per-row inline rename affordance to the chat session-picker popover. An edit button on each row opens an inline input seeded with the current label; Enter / Save commits, Escape / Cancel discards.
- **Reuse, no new write path:** Commit goes through the existing self-refreshing `patchSession(state, key, {label})` flow (the same `sessions.patch` + `loadSessions()` path the admin Sessions tab already uses). Empty/whitespace input clears the label via `normalizeOptionalString`, matching admin-tab semantics exactly.
- **Out of scope (intentional):** chat slash-commands for naming (`/name`, `/new --name`, `/sessions`), switching sessions from text channels, and any LLM auto-suggest. Those are planned as separate, smaller PRs.
- **Success:** Users can rename a session without leaving the chat; behavior and validation are identical to the existing admin rename.

## Linked context

Related #54397, #85502

This is the small "header/sidebar shortcut" that maintainers explicitly invited as a separate polish request when closing #85502 (the admin-tab rename shipped there). It also advances the chat-native session-management UX discussed in #54397. I deliberately did not put `Closes #` because neither issue is a 1:1 match — happy to add one if a maintainer prefers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** Rename a session inline from the in-chat session picker (open picker → edit icon on a row → type a new label → Enter/Save), including clear-on-empty and Cancel/Escape.
- **Real environment tested:** A live self-hosted OpenClaw gateway (v2026.5.27, Node v22, on WSL2/Ubuntu-24.04) serving this PR branch's built `control-ui`, driven through a real Chromium browser via Playwright. The browser authenticates against the running gateway over the actual WebSocket control channel and exercises the production render path end to end. Endpoint, tokens, and other session labels are redacted.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - Built this branch's `control-ui` and pointed the running gateway at it; confirmed it was live with `curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz` → `200`.
  - Drove the real UI with a Playwright script (`node rename_e2e.js`, headless Chromium): connect to the gateway → open the in-chat session picker → click a row's inline edit icon → type a new label → press Enter to commit → re-read the session list to confirm persistence → restore the original label.
- **Evidence after fix (browser console + control-channel capture):**

  ```
  $ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:<port>/healthz   ->   200
  $ node rename_e2e.js   (headless Chromium driving the live gateway)
  [control-ui] rpc method=connect           ok=true   auth=password
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.subscribe ok=true
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true
  picker opened -> inline edit icon present on each session row
  entered inline edit mode -> typed a new label -> pressed Enter
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.patch    ok=true   durationMs=69
  [control-ui] rpc method=sessions.list     ok=true   (self-refresh)
  verified: the new label is rendered in the picker row AND in the
           left sidebar "Recent" list, with no page reload
  cleanup: original label restored -> sessions.patch ok=true
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Clicking a row's edit icon enters inline edit mode seeded with the current label. Pressing Enter commits through the existing `sessions.patch` flow (`ok=true`) and the self-refreshing `sessions.list` immediately shows the new name in both the picker row and the sidebar "Recent" list — no page reload and without opening the admin Sessions tab. Cancel/Escape and closing the picker mid-edit discard the draft and leave the label unchanged. The new name persisted across a fresh `sessions.list` read; the original label was then restored.
- **What was not tested:** Non-web channels (this is a Control UI surface only), and exact visual pixel styling across physical devices/browsers other than headless Chromium. The commit path is the same `patchSession` flow the admin Sessions tab already uses, which is additionally covered by the DOM-level regression tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`.

## Tests and validation

- Added 4 focused tests in `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts` under "chat session controls": commit via Enter, clear-on-whitespace via Save, cancel via Escape, and discard-on-close (regression guard so closing the picker mid-edit cannot leak a stale rename/draft).
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`, `pnpm ui:i18n:check`, oxlint and oxfmt all green on the changes.

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change?** Yes — a new rename control in the chat session picker.
- **Config/env/migration change?** No.
- **Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-exec change?** No new write path or permission surface; reuses the existing `sessions.patch` flow already used by the admin rename.
- **Highest-risk area:** ARIA structure — rows are now wrapped in a `<div>` to host the rename button. Mitigation: wrappers are marked `role="presentation"` so options remain owned by the `listbox`. Note: this picker was already a loose listbox (button options, no roving tabindex); a deeper `list`/`listitem` refactor is intentionally left out of scope.
- **Mitigation:** Label normalization/clear semantics are delegated to the same helper as the admin tab; commit uses the existing self-refreshing controller; rename state is cleared on every picker-close path (covered by a test).

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer review of the UX placement and the ARIA `role="presentation"` approach.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** N/A (initial submission).

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver — returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver — skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: 17 tests (5 new — setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound)
- `channel.test.ts`: 2 tests (new file — onReady drain, onResumed drain)

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw `2026.6.2` (commit `b90683e`, branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials (appId `<redacted>`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built patched OpenClaw from fix branch: `pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts`
  2. Installed globally: `npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz` — confirmed version: `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)`
  3. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  4. Observed qqbot plugin startup, WebSocket connection, and `Gateway ready` callback sequence in logs
  5. Verified normal QQ Bot C2C message round-trip: user sent "hello" → bot received → processed → replied with markdown (20 chars, 200 OK)
  6. Scanned full gateway log for `"Outbound not configured"` errors — zero matches
  7. Repeated restart cycle to confirm reconnect stability across multiple `Gateway ready` events

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Patched OpenClaw installed and running
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)

  # Gateway restart sequence — qqbot reconnects cleanly
  2026-06-02T22:38:53.727+08:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; restarting
  2026-06-02T22:39:15.986+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.6s)
  2026-06-02T22:39:16.171+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — appId=<redacted>, enabled=true
  2026-06-02T22:39:17.387+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Connecting to wss://api.sgroup.qq.com/websocket
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.468+08:00 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.641+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready

  # Successful QQ Bot message delivery after reconnect
  2026-06-02T22:32:40.229+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Processing message from <redacted>: hello
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.431+08:00 [qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.432+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Sent markdown chunk (20/20 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

  # Zero outbound resolution errors across entire log history
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured.*qqbot" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Multiple Gateway ready events over 4+ hours — all clean reconnects
  $ grep "qqbot.*Gateway ready" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log | wc -l
  18
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia` methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. Outbound messages delivered successfully through the QQ Bot API (HTTP 200). Zero occurrences of the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error across 18 reconnect cycles over a 4+ hour observation window. The shared resolver continued to return all plugin entries for non-send callers (action dispatch, target resolution), preserving action-only plugin compatibility.

- **What was not tested**:

  Live drain proof is not reproducible on the fixed build. The `drainReconnectPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2) was verified via unit tests but not observed draining actual queued entries in a live environment. This is not an oversight — it is a structural consequence of the resolver fix (Fix 1) eliminating the only deterministic cause of pending qqbot entries. Code-level analysis confirms this:

  **How the delivery queue works** (`deliver.ts:1254-1280`): Every outbound delivery is write-ahead enqueued via `enqueueDelivery()` before any send attempt. On success, `ackDelivery()` removes the entry (`deliver.ts:1367`). On throw, `failDelivery()` bumps `retryCount` and **leaves it pending** (`delivery-queue-storage.ts:137-144`).

  **What created the pending entries in #88955**: The `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` throw fired from `createChannelHandler` (`deliver.ts:223-231`) when `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery` returned a setup-only plugin with no send methods. This throw occurs **outside** the per-payload try/catch (`deliver.ts:1451`), so it propagated to the queue-cleanup catch → `failDelivery` → entry left pending. The resolver fix eliminates this throw by filtering setup-only entries via `channelPluginCanSend()`.

  **Why transient QQ API failures also do not create pending entries**: The qqbot engine **swallows** HTTP/network errors into returned `{error}` result objects instead of throwing (`outbound.ts:301-304, 313-318`). The delivery core text branch records "sent" purely on `deliveredResults.length > 0` with no message-id identity gate (`deliver.ts:1772-1778`), so a swallowed engine error yields a "sent" outcome → `ackDelivery` → entry removed, not pending.

  **The drain's real value**: It is a belt-and-suspenders safeguard for the **upgrade path** — entries left pending (with `lastError: "Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`) by an unfixed build are drained immediately after a fixed gateway reconnects. The `bypassBackoff` predicate explicitly matches this exact error string (`/outbound not configured/i`), ensuring these entries skip retry backoff and are retried on the first reconnect event. If the retry still fails (e.g., the plugin is genuinely misconfigured), `isPermanentDeliveryError` (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:79`) moves the entry to `failed/` — no infinite retry loop. `MAX_RETRIES` is also enforced before the bypass branch (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:544`). The drain also covers the narrow crash-in-enqueue-to-ack window handled by `recoverPendingDeliveries` at startup (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`). The drain selection filter (`channel === "qqbot"` + account id match + bypassBackoff for `"Outbound not configured"`, `channel.test.ts:90-152`) and the reconnect-drain integration logic (`delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`) are exercised by 35 unit tests (all passing).

  **Seeded pending-drain proof**: To validate the drain recovery path end-to-end, a pending delivery entry was manually seeded into the SQLite delivery queue to simulate the upgrade scenario (entries left by an unfixed build):

  1. Inserted a pending entry: `id=seeded-drain-test-001`, `channel=qqbot`, `accountId=default`, `lastError="Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`, `retry_count=1`
  2. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Observed recovery sequence in logs:

  ```
  00:30:00.353 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.9s)
  00:30:00.987 [delivery-recovery] Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
  00:30:01.567 [qqbot] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Recovered delivery seeded-drain-test-001 on qqbot
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Delivery recovery complete: 1 recovered, 0 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
  00:30:02.816 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  00:30:02.952 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready
  ```

  4. Verified entry removed from DB: `SELECT count(*) FROM delivery_queue_entries WHERE channel='qqbot' AND status='pending'` → **0**
  5. Zero `"Outbound not configured"` errors after restart

  **Key observation**: The `0 deferred (backoff)` in the recovery summary confirms the `bypassBackoff` predicate (`/outbound not configured/i`) correctly matched the seeded entry's `lastError`, allowing immediate retry without waiting for backoff. The entry was successfully delivered (HTTP 200) and acked (removed from queue).

  **Note**: The startup `recoverPendingDeliveries` path (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`) executed before the qqbot `onReady` callback (`00:30:00.987` vs `00:30:02.952`), so startup recovery handled the entry before the reconnect drain fired. Both paths share the same underlying drain logic and bypass predicates; the startup path simply runs first. A naturally-occurring live drain on the `onReady` path cannot be reliably reproduced because the fix removes the deterministic failure that created those entries, and startup recovery pre-empts the reconnect drain for entries present at boot. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.
